---
title: "Welcome to the College of Idaho!"
subtitle: "Data Science Activity"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    logo: style/Howler-Logo-01.png
    css: styles.css
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    center: false
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: "style/slide_images/image2.jpeg"
      data-background-opacity: "0.6"
      data-background-color: "#000000"
---

## Who Am I?

::: incremental
- Eric Friedlander, Assistant Professor of Computer Science
- Ph.D. in Statistics & Operations Research (UNC-Chapel Hill) — I studied **queueing theory**: the math of how things wait in line
- Queueing shows up everywhere: hospitals, call centers, airports, 911 dispatch
- Today we'll use it to solve a life-or-death problem
:::

## Activity Time!!!

::: incremental
- Our setting: **there's been a major explosion in the library!**
- About 50 patients with a variety of symptoms
- Only 10 ambulances on scene (although more are on the way)
- You can't save everyone — how do you decide who goes first?
:::

## Triage: Sorting Patients by Priority

- Based on a patient's symptoms, you'll classify them into one of four categories:
  - **Red**: Highest priority for an ambulance
  - **Yellow**: Medium priority for an ambulance
  - **Green**: Lowest priority for an ambulance
  - **Black (Expectant)**: **No ambulance is sent at all**

::: {.fragment}
If a patient waits too long for transport — or is marked Black — **they die.**
:::

## Why Would We Ever Choose Black?

::: incremental
- Black isn't "giving up" — it's realism
- Some symptoms are so severe that no realistic response time can save that patient
- Sending them an ambulance anyway just means **someone savable waits longer instead**
- The hardest, most important skill in mass-casualty triage: knowing when Black is the correct call
:::

## Your Job

- Develop a **triage policy**: a rule that maps each symptom to Red, Yellow, Green, or Black
- You won't be told which symptoms are truly dangerous — you have to figure it out from the data
- Run the simulation, see who died, and refine your policy

## Let's Walk Through the App

- [Open the triage simulator](https://efriedlander.shinyapps.io/high-school-triage-activity/)
- Two tabs: **Triage Station** (set up your policy) and **After Action Report** (see what happened)

## How to Use the App

::: incremental
1. On **Triage Station**, assign a color to every symptom (all start as Green)
2. Set "Number of Simulations" to **Statistical Run (100)** and click **Run Simulation**
3. You'll land on **After Action Report** — check **Average Deaths**
4. Check **The Deadly Symptoms** chart: which symptoms killed the most patients?
5. Check **Triage vs. Transport Time** — use the **Symptom** dropdown to zoom in on one symptom at a time: what color did it get, and how long did it wait?
6. Go back to **Triage Station**, adjust your colors, and run it again
:::

## Design Against 100 Runs, Not 1

::: incremental
- **Single Run** shows you one random group of patients — you could just get lucky (or unlucky)
- **Statistical Run (100)** tests your policy against 100 different random groups
- Use the **average, min, and max deaths** to judge your policy — a good policy does well most of the time, not just once
- Use Single Run only to eyeball individual patients — design your policy off the 100-run numbers
:::

## Competition Time

- You have the rest of the time to refine your policy
- At the very end, I will give you a "seed" and you'll initiate a single run
- The team with the fewest deaths wins!
- Who can design the best triage policy?
